key_insight: The paper extends compatible-strain mixed finite elements from simplicial to general quadrilateral meshes by building shape functions directly in physical space, enabling first-order formulations for both compressible and incompressible 2D nonlinear elasticity.
engineering_relevance: This gives solid-mechanics analysts a more flexible mesh option with fewer degrees of freedom while preserving the structure needed for accurate nonlinear elasticity simulations.
method: Use first-kind Nedelec interpolation for the displacement gradient on quadrilateral elements.
Construct stress shape functions compatible with displacement and gradient discretizations.
Avoid Piola-based mapping on general quads by computing shape functions directly in physical space; condense pressure locally for incompressible cases.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports numerical examples showing the quadrilateral elements can solve problems that previously required second-order simplicial compatible-strain elements, but with fewer degrees of freedom.
watch_item: The approach depends on direct physical-space shape-function construction and careful element-level implementation, so robustness and efficiency may be sensitive to mesh geometry and quadrature accuracy.
paper_title: First-Order Compatible-Strain Mixed Quadrilateral Finite Elements for 2D Nonlinear Elasticity
